subject: Chemistry
title: Why Coins Turn Green
description: A fast breakdown of Why Coins Turn Green for builders.

hook.text: Old copper coins, the Statue of Liberty, and church domes all turn green for the same chemical reason.

buildup.text: Copper reacts with oxygen in air to form a thin layer of copper oxide—a dark brown coating.

discovery.text: Over time, copper oxide reacts further with CO₂ and moisture to form copper carbonate—a green mineral called patina.

twist.text: The Statue of Liberty was originally copper-brown. It took about 20 years for the green patina to fully develop.

climax.text: Patina actually protects the copper beneath from further corrosion. The green layer is self-defense.

punchline.text: Copper plus air plus time equals green. The patina is armor, not damage.

quiz.question: What causes the green color on old copper objects?
options: Copper carbonate (patina) formed by reaction with air, CO₂, and moisture
Algae growing on the metal surface
